title: ISMS Policy Acknowledgment and Version Drift in Confluence or SharePoint

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.9: ISMS Policy Acknowledgment and Version Drift in Confluence or SharePoint.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.9' running on confluence:audit or ms:o365:management SharePoint, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Atlassian Jira/Confluence HEC or O365
spl:
index=collab sourcetype=confluence:audit earliest=-365d object_type=page (space_key=ISMS OR match(object_name,"(?i)information.security.policy"))
| stats latest(_time) as last_change, count as views by object_name
| eval days_since_change=round((now()-last_change)/86400,0)
description: Shows when information security policy documents were last updated and accessed so management direction stays current and employees can evidence review.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes collab and sourcetypes confluence:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map document IDs to Annex A scope; (2) set alert if no update in 365 days; (3) export for management review minutes. Indexes required: `index=collab`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=confluence:audit`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for shows when information security policy documents were last updated and accessed so management direction stays current and employees can evidence review. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Atlassian Jira/Confluence HEC or O365.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `confluence:audit` or `ms:o365:management` SharePoint.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map document IDs to Annex A scope; (2) set alert if no update in 365 days; (3) export for management review minutes.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=collab sourcetype=confluence:audit earliest=-365d object_type=page (space_key=ISMS OR match(object_name,"(?i)information.security.policy"))
| stats latest(_time) as last_change, count as views by object_name
| eval days_since_change=round((now()-last_change)/86400,0)
```

Understanding this SPL

**ISMS Policy Acknowledgment and Version Drift in Confluence or SharePoint (A.5.1)** — Shows when information security policy documents were last updated and accessed so management direction stays current and employees can evidence review.

Documented **Data sources**: `confluence:audit` or `ms:o365:management` SharePoint. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Atlassian Jira/Confluence HEC or O365. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: collab; **sourcetype**: confluence:audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=collab, sourcetype=confluence:audit, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by object_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_since_change** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Single value
